turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: We'll hear arguments next in 1439, Hughes against Oklahoma. Mr. Helton you may proceed whenever you are ready.

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, may it please the Court. This case is before you on appeal from the Court of Criminal Appeals of the State of Oklahoma in which the appellant was convicted of violating the following section of the Oklahoma Wildlife Code, specifically Section 4-115 (B) which provides in pertinent part as follows. No person may transport or ship minnows for sale outside the state, which were seined or procured from the waters of this state except that; First; nothing contained herein shall prohibit any person from leaving the state possessing three dozen or less minnows. Number two; nothing contained herein shall prohibit sale and shipment of minnows raised in a regularly licensed commercial minnow hatchery. It is my position here in support of the proposition that the subject law is repugnant to the Commerce Clause of the United States. Primarily, if it pleased the Court, based upon the decision of this Court of this past term in City of Philadelphia versus New Jersey --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACKMUN
turns.text: Mr. Helton, I take it you are relying solely on the Commerce Clause?

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, no other.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACKMUN
turns.text: You are not relying on the Privileges and Immunities Clause or anything else. Just --

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: That is right.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACKMUN
turns.text: You put your case in the Commerce Clause?

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: Yes sir. No other constitutional ground do I position at all. That is to say that in as much as this law is 100% on its face blocked or curtained against interstate commerce, that it constitutes therefore that discriminatory nature, that disruption of the right to engage in interstate commerce by the other states and their citizens, particularly within the rule of this Court in the City of Philadelphia versus New Jersey, wherein Mr. Justice Stewart writing for your Court, said that; whenever the discrimination reached certain -- when it was that degree of discrimination, that it just constitutes a barrier of curtained interstate commerce, it’s unnecessary, it's immaterial to determine whether or not the state in enacting its law properly exercised its police power.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: What if Oklahoma passed a statute that had as its preamble or recital that the supply of buffalo if they have any or elk if they have any being limited, there could only be one taken by any person within a certain limited season and none could be taken outside of the State?

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: Alright sir. And sir, Oklahoma did that in its conservation law in Section 116-A, the very following section that follows this section and I think that's a perfectly valid and legitimate law.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Fine.

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: The difference sir in this case and the supposed case that you gave me is the party here complaining is not the party with the right of the access. So in this case, Fred Shellkey (ph) is the man who took the minnows from the water of the State. Mr. Hughes bought the minnows from Mr. Shellkey under this law.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: But under this particular statute that before us now there is no prohibition against his buying, but there is a prohibition against his taking them out of the state, is there not?

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: That is right sir.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: How do you distinguish that from taking elk out of the states?

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: The difference between the right to take in my judgment is the State very likely in fact I think without question, the State has and is properly exercising its police power when it controls the access to its wildlife as distinguished from controlling the right to, that is prohibiting the right to transport. When at the same time they never prohibited, they never in any manner prohibited and attempted to control a disposition of it, the commerce in it from their license taker to the next party.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well so that, under the law you may commercialize with -- was natural minnows as long as you transport and sell them inside the State. You can take natural minnows and sell them within the State of Oklahoma?

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: In my -- I don't think there is any question about -- you are asking about the right of Oklahoma to limit that.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: No, I am asking if I'm a dealer in minnows, I can buy natural minnows or I can go seine them myself and sell them to anybody I want to inside the state?

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: You are right.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: And transport them anywhere I want to inside the State for sale?

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: But I can't transport them across the boundary for sale?

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACKMUN
turns.text: Or he can destroy them if you chooses, burn them up?

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: I am sorry sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACKMUN
turns.text: I say or he can destroy them if he chooses or burn them up as long as he does it within the state of Oklahoma?

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: That would be right. What I really, what I think that would help us is to understand that this overall conversation code of Oklahoma divides the commerce into two steps. There is a division in the commerce and the first step with the commerce is from the water to the licensed, the party authorized to take them out of the water. That's step number one, our commerce and if the court pleas that's internal commerce. What Texas people or Texas as a (Inaudible) would have any interest in that, they would have none whatever. But now there is a second step in the commerce and that second step is when the authorized taker sells and disposes and Oklahoma, and at that point because sir, because Oklahoma never put any kind of restriction on his right to -- the second step of that commerce is 100% unregulated by Oklahoma. Oklahoma has not even enacted anything other than this one section, that deals with that second step of commerce.

turns.name: JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: But that regulates, doesn't it, that one section that you just referred to?

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: It does sir, but and I don't disagree with it. The very object of it though is to prevent the exportation, but the very object of it at that point since Oklahoma never by any other law limited that second step in any manner, never controlled it in any manner. At that point, it had become inter-state commerce and at that point this law then has put the curtain that it cannot be.

turns.name: JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: You say then that Oklahoma could prohibit any sort of commercial dealing in natural minnows?

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: I believe they can sir, provided, provided that they make the requisite necessary finding that's the basis of their exercising their police power.

turns.name: JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: Well, I thought the police power is always presumed to have been properly exercised unless your state --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: States very seldom --

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: I don't understand it. It's always presumed.

turns.name: JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: Well, aren't all state laws presumed constitutional?

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: I am aware of the utterances to that effect, but I also I am aware that wherever a law shows on its face for example and refutes that presumption, then the presumption is refused. Now then, if I be wrong, if I'm wrong in my position that is if I'm wrong in my position that the real of City of Philadelphia didn't apply for this case for example. If I'm wrong in that, which I strongly believe I am not, but if I'm wrong then the next question that our cases teaches and tell us is that in the event that a law enacted under the police power is going to interfere with inter-state commerce. There is a question of whether or not it's, the question is then is it doing it incidentally. In other words, though there be a valid exercise of the police power in connection with the operation of that valid exercise that is there an incidental effect upon inter-state commerce and what is law in that respect. Well, one of the -- at that point, it has recently been determined by this Court that the burden shifts upon the state to justify its own use, I mean, its police power use and in its justification, it must among things, show that it could not use its police power in a different manner, thereby reducing the burdens upon inter-state commerce. Now then in this case, if we agree that the object of this law, which I find nothing in this law that supports this object either expressly or inferentially, but the Oklahoma high court did. It said the purpose was to prevent depletion of minnows by commercial exportation or importation. Oklahoma did not, but could have put catch limits on its minnows. Oklahoma could have, but did not make any control over how many minnows the dealers would have in possession at one time.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Could they put a limit on how many could be transported over the state line?

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: They certainly could in my opinion, yes sir.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Haven't they done so indirectly here?

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: No sir. For the reason that - well the real object of the three dozen rules, we got a lake down there between Texas and Oklahoma. The Red River runs through and that, the three dozen rule in my opinion had reference to that lake.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Texoma I assume it is called.

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: The part of the lake --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: The lake is is called Texoma.

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: Yes sir, that's the name of it Your honor, it sure is. Now then --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: But what was the purpose, what do you suggest was the purpose of the three dozen limit?

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: To take care of lake Texoma.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Well.

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: In other words, I'm a Texan and I --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Is there anything in the statute about that?

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: I beg your pardon.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Anything in the statute about that?

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: No, sir, no.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: What do you suggest on its face the three dozen limits implies or suggests?

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: Well, if the --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: You want to hold down.

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: There really isn't anything from the law that we could tell anything Your Honor.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Isn't it the same kind of limitation that you put, that you can only take six trout or five pheasants or whatever?

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: Well, I feel this way that --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Isn't it a conservation measure?

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: I'm unable to read a conservation measure in the three dozen.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Well, isn't three dozen about what you might mean for a day of fishing, to catch whatever limits Oklahoma has. Isn't there a reasonable possibility that there's a relationship?

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: Well, my trouble sir is I fish too much down there and I can understand the relationship of three dozen to us local boys that fish now --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: You don't have –-

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: Now that's not in this record and I understand that.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: You don't really need it --

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: Well to me --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: More than three dozen in a day do you?

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: It is impertinent sir. I know there is no -- three dozen couldn't have anything to do with conservation of the minnows in Oklahoma or at least that's my opinion it being there. It's not supported by the records, but that's – if I maybe indulged then I will say that.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Mr. Helton I didn't understand, but I guess I'm just stupid, but what is the reference to lake Texoma? Why would this problem --

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: Alright sir. On Red River sir, there's a lake that dammed up that river and thus the water that rivers in both states.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: So consequently, I think the legislature had in mind, if a Texas fisherman came over into Oklahoma to buy him some minnows and does go back on the Texas side of the lake to fish without this exception, he can be a violator of this law.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Well, why don't you make him fish on the Oklahoma side of the lake you know that.

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: Oh that's terrific. I hadn't thought about that; that would be one effect of it. You make him fish for he didn't want a fish.

turns.name: JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: Of course, if they want to make him fish on the Oklahoma side of the lake they would have no exception at all?

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: If I listen to you sir, you're meaning to infer that -- if they wanted to leave the three dozen exception acts --

turns.name: JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: They'd force him to fish.

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: The effect would be the Texan people fish on the Oklahoma side. Yes sir, I think that would be right. Now then -- I naturally, there is, after we read the residue of this law that is other than this Section 4-115 (A) or 115 (B), other than that one law, there isn't a single law in this code that directs itself toward any kind of general welfare protection, that is protection of Oklahoma minnows. It's totally barren of any kind of the law, because it's totally barren of any kind of law, it automatically, the only thing you can decide is that they wanted to give the (Inaudible) an economic benefit that it's a discriminatory law that discriminated in favor of the (Inaudible) business. The other thing about the law that's to foster and carry out and further the general welfare of power of the people of Oklahoma.

turns.name: JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: Mr. Helton, could I ask you one question before you sit down. Do you think it would be necessary if we were to rule in your favor for us to expressly overrule the case of Geer against Connecticut?

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: Well, sir, I -- no sir I'm really, yes and no, if I may. I'll answer yes part first and then no part second. First sir, the statue in the Geer case is basically and fundamentally different in this Oklahoma Statute. The difference is this. Geer directed the prohibition to the very act of taking, the word Geer statue says it is hereby prohibited for anyone to kill our wild life and remove them from the state. Now I haven't quoted it to you verbatim, but I haven't deviated that. Now this law, unlike Geer isn't directed to the taker. The actual statutes aren't connected with the power or the access to take. That distinguishes Geer --

turns.name: JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: But it makes the Oklahoma Statute narrower than the one in here, doesn't it?

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: Sir I am sorry.

turns.name: JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: Doesn't it make the Oklahoma Statute narrower than the one in Geer?

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: Narrower?

turns.name: JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: Because Oklahoma Statute regulates less conduct than the Connecticut Statute did in Geer?

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: Well sir to me the Geer statute, the emphasis is on the initial right to take. The Oklahoma Statute doesn't focus its emphasis there. It focuses trying to enforce something that's not visible in the law. Now then thus, now then I said that yes and then no, now then the other part, the other side of the coin is to me this Court in the last term, in the last recent terms, in Baldwin versus Montana and all the cases you've stated there, limited to interstate commerce only. I think that you have the -- I think you have more or less terminated now the ownership doctrine. I am not talking about that you've terminated the power of the states under the police power. You said the majority opinion relative to the ownership of wild game in the states was error.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Mr. Bruce?

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice may it please the Court. In order to focus upon Oklahoma's argument before this Court, I feel it incumbent upon me to bring to the Court's attention the posture of the appellant's argument in the court's decision below. The posture of the argument below are the appellant was in the form of distinguishing Geer versus Connecticut from the case of Foster-Fountain Packing Company versus Haydel. Now as this Court is aware the Geer decision involved wildlife. The statute there prohibited the transport of that wildlife across the state line. In Foster-Fountain Packing Company versus Haydel, this Court looked at the underlying fact situation involved in that case where the Louisiana Statute in the name of conservation prohibited the exportation of shrimp in which the hose and heads had not been removed, thereby requiring their removal in the home state of Louisiana. This Court held in that case that the purpose and effect of that statute was economic protectionism for Louisiana residents. That is, that statute favored Louisiana simply because by the very nature of the statute, the packing was required to be done in Louisiana to the exclusion of other states. Now in the lower court as I mentioned, the appellant distinguished Geer versus Connecticut from Foster-Fountain Packing Company versus Haydel and relied up on the Foster case stating that it was applicable to this case. The posture, however on appeal by the appellant here is that as I read his brief that Geer versus Connecticut is no longer viable when subjected to a Commerce Clause tag. It's the view of Oklahoma that the concept in Geer is still viable today. The concept of the state's interest, the state's interest in controlling its wildlife is as much as alive today as it was in the time when Geer versus Connecticut was rendered.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACKMUN
turns.text: What in your opinion Mr. Attorney General is the justification for the Oklahoma statute?

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: As a conservation measure, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACKMUN
turns.text: Well, do you agree with counsel on the other side that a Texas resident could go into Oklahoma and buy minnows and burn them up in front of the commercial establishment from which he bought them?

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: I would agree if there is no statutory prohibition.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACKMUN
turns.text: That there's no wildlife conservation.

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: No, sir, the statute does not address that, that possibility notion.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACKMUN
turns.text: Well, if that's a possibility, why they would be characterized as a conservation measure?

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: Well, as argued by the appellant below in which the state concedes the only viable commercial use for minnows is as bait for other fish. It serves no other purpose as far as man is concerned in his commercial interest. Of course the state maintains that minnows also serve other important functions of course as a natural food supply for game fish and that the balance of the – for the maintaining of the balance of the minnow population in the natural streams.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: For ducks and birds and a lot of others too, don't they--?

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: -- live off of minnows.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACKMUN
turns.text: Isn't that interest served just as much on the Texas side, of the lake that straddles the state boundary?

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: Are you speaking of Lake Texoma Mr. Justice Blackmun?

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACKMUN
turns.text: Whatever the lake is?

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: Yes, I think that interest would serve Texas as well as Oklahoma.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACKMUN
turns.text: Wouldn't it serve just as much on a lake ten miles inside Texas as Oklahoma lake?

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: I am not sure I understand your question sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACKMUN
turns.text: Well, suppose that Mr. Hughes here took his minnows and sold them to Texas fishermen on a lake ten miles inside the Texas boundary, isn't that natural preservation purpose just as well served there, or are you restricting it entirely to Oklahoma nature?

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: Well, I would concede that certainly that would serve Texas interests, but such wholesale exploitation of minnows, natural minnows in Oklahoma streams, if Oklahoma permitted the export, it would require Oklahoma to not only keep track of what's happening, but to make sure that additional minnows were added to make up for those which were exported.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACKMUN
turns.text: But at the same time though, I could go into Oklahoma and buy them and bring them up.

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACKMUN
turns.text: And there is no statute that would prevent me from doing so?

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Maybe the next legislature counsel may take that into account and make a standard prohibition?

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: Well sir, we've never had that type of problem in Oklahoma?[Laughter]

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: I doubt if you will either.

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: Now one another case citied by the appellant the City of Philadelphia versus New Jersey of course a recent case by this Court regarding a dispute between New Jersey and other states regarding the land -- for use of landfill sites. In my brief, I distinguish that case as I did other cases in that, in that case it involved a discrimination provision, in other words the State of New Jersey discriminated against out of state citizens as opposed to citizens of its own state.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Also the subject of a commerce wasn't wildlife?

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: That's correct Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: The Geer case didn't have anything to do with that case?

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: Yes sir and as I pointed out in my brief or hopefully I pointed out in my brief that wildlife is a unique area of law, and that the state has special interest in preserving the wildlife.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: So you -- do you think in serving that in you can permit commercialization with the same natural minnows within the state, but prevent their export?

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Just as a way of limiting the take?

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: A way of conservation yes Mr. Justice White.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Even though there are no limits on the amount or number of minnows that your own people can take?

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: I might say that I believe other states do use the possession limit as a means of protecting their interest as an advice to --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Anybody, any out of staters who come to Oklahoma, they are free to buy minnows and use them in Oklahoma waters.

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: Yes Mr. Justice White they are.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Your Oklahoma supply people are -- then have -- they have the exclusive on the sale of naturally seined minnows?

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: Well, they are licensed by the state, yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: Well, there is no prohibition against buying minnows in Texas and bringing them into Oklahoma and using them as a bait, is there?

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: There is a prohibition on buying Oklahoma minnows?

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: Well, that's the focus of our statute in issue here. There isn't an Oklahoma provision regarding an import license.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Now we got -- what's there about that?

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: But that's not the focus of the inquiry here.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: But what about it then? Can I bring minnows from out of the state and use them in the Oklahoma waters?

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: The statute provides a procedure for that.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: For the licensing?

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: You don't object to commercial raising of minnows and taking them?

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: No.

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: Commercial hatchery of minnows, no Mr. Justice Marshall.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: You don't object to people taking them and seining them either do you as such?

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: No sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Because this man here was a professional (Inaudible). That was his business of seining wild minnows.

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: So If you are allowed that unlimited, how are you preserving wildlife or protecting wildlife –

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: The very –

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: -- the unlimited.

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: Alright sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: The taking is unlimited, right?

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: And sold it off.

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: As to the number taken, no sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: Mr. Justice Marshall in response to your inquiry, the -- it's true that Oklahoma has no numerical limits on the actual taking of minnows from its natural streams. There is really no need for that in light of the exportation law that is since as appellant and appellee both agree that the only commercial interest in minnows is as bait, that's the sole commercial interest of minnows. Then those that are seined from Oklahoma streams are returned to Oklahoma streams in the form of bait. So it does -- there is really no need for --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Supposed they were returned to the streams in a state above Oklahoma, wouldn't they in turn return to Oklahoma?

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: Well, if it adjoined, if the stream ran into Oklahoma, yes sir, conceivably they would.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: So that's not the only point.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Mr. Bruce when they returned to the streams as bait, their life expectancy isn't very long.[Laughter]

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: Well, Mr. Justice Stevens I won't debate. I am not a fisherman unlike Mr. Helton I'm not a --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: The minnow's life expectancy isn't the longest anyhow?

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Would you take the position that Oklahoma could have a statute that said that any fish caught in Oklahoma shall be consumed in Oklahoma, isn't that what the statute, with respect to one time?

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: Yes, yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: So they could say any fish caught here have to be consumed, cannot be shipped out of state? That's the same issue, isn't it?

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Just like Geer?

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: We are not – we were discussing in response to some questions the fact there is no limit on how much you can – what quantity of natural minnows can be seined within the state, but the only purpose, the rational purpose of seining within the state would be for a dealer to sell them under that three dozen limit to other fishermen is that not so? You wouldn't go about seining fish just for the--?

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: For recreational purposes, no Mr. Chief Justice.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: -- they're not edible in that sense, and will be pretty expensive fertilizer, you would only be seining them to probably to sell them three dozen at a time to fishermen?

turns.name: BILL J. BRUCE
turns.text: That's correct Your Honor. The statutory scheme is self executing so to speak that the natural and practical commercial object of minnows serves as a natural limitation. If the Oklahoma bars the exportation, then they would be returned to Oklahoma streams in the form of bait. Thank you.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Do you any further, Mr. Helton?

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: Your Honor, I'm just -- in answer to Mr. Justice White's question to counsel in Section 4-1159(A) it provides, “No person may ship or transport minnows for sale into this state from an outside source without having first procured a license for such from the Director. So if you come to Oklahoma fishing from your native state Colorado, don't bring any minnows.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Without a license.

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: And you can't get one, now let's talk about minnow dealer license and you can't get a minnow dealer's license.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: They just --

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: -- unless you are a resident of Oklahoma.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Alright.

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: So I am pointing out to you that nobody can come into Oklahoma to fish and bring their minnows into Oklahoma.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: And so no out of state minnow dealer, fellows who would like to sell you the fishing equipment can send you any minnows from outside the state for two reasons. One, you can't get any Oklahoma minnows--

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: And then of course for an outsider that wanted to bring minnows into Oklahoma --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Can't get the license.

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: -- to feed the fish in Oklahoma, couldn't do it.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: Well could you read that language again Mr. Helton. I wasn't sure from its literal language that it applied to someone who wasn't trying to sell them in Oklahoma?

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: “No person may ship or transport minnows for sale” you were right sir, yes sir I am sorry sir you can do it

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: I may tempt to bring my minnows into Oklahoma.

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: Yes sir, okay you are right Mr. Justice --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: As long as he goes into --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACKMUN
turns.text: That's a great relief to Mr. Justice White.[Laughter]

turns.name: ROBERT M. HELTON
turns.text: Yeah. Thank you gentlemen.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Thank you gentlemen. The case is submitted.
